imageCaption: Aaliyah stands before the Cordoba Cathedral's rose window, which displays layered temporal frequencies—each petal shows a different moment from the family's journey, all resonating at once.
text:
The Córdoba Cathedral rose window was doing something that rose windows were categorically not supposed to do, which was displaying mathematical data through the medium of colored light.

Aaliyah stood directly in front of it while her family spread out through the cathedral like they were all simultaneously praying and taking an exam they hadn't studied for. The window showed concentric circles of frequency—each ring a different color, each color representing a different moment from their journey. She could see the day they left Lisbon (red, anxious). Barcelona (orange, confused but pushing forward). Seville (gold, beginning to understand). And underneath it all, a baseline frequency that seemed to be the color of home, which apparently was a very specific shade of deep blue that Aaliyah's brain was interpreting as the color of Curaçao even though she'd never been there.

"This is definitely what ARIA-7 meant by 'device,'" Elijah called from somewhere near the altar. He was on his back, apparently examining the cathedral ceiling for glitch activity. "It's measuring us. Look, the petals are getting brighter."

He was right. The window was brightening, the colors intensifying, as though the rose itself was becoming more present in their timeline. As though their proximity to it was making it more real, or as though their frequency was making it more visible, or both simultaneously, which was increasingly just how things worked now.

Luisana was writing frantically in her journal, capturing the light patterns. Anthony was slowly walking the perimeter of the cathedral, documenting everything with his phone camera—which meant either it would work perfectly or the temporal distortion would turn every photo into abstract art. There was no in-between.

Sully had trotted directly to a specific corner of the cathedral and was sitting with an expression of profound purpose.

"Sully says this is the center," Elijah reported. He'd abandoned the ceiling examination and was making his way over to the dog. "The exact point where our frequency is strongest."

It was a corner near what looked like a centuries-old piece of stonework. There was nothing special about it from any normal architectural standpoint—no altar, no artifact, no obvious reason it should be the focal point of anything. But when Aaliyah walked toward it, she felt it. A vibration in the air, in her chest, in the very particular frequency of her own heartbeat. As though the cathedral itself had learned to resonate at the same pitch her family did.

"This is where the cathedral was built," her mother said, reading from what appeared to be a placard she'd discovered. "This exact corner. This was where someone laid the first stone, what, eight hundred years ago. Someone made a choice to start here, and everything else grew from this point."

"The first heartbeat," Aaliyah said slowly. "They didn't know what they were doing. They just... felt like it was the right place."

The window brightened again.

And then something else happened, which was that Aaliyah's phone buzzed with a notification that shouldn't exist. There were no cell towers in actively fragmenting temporal zones. There was no signal. And yet:

*Frequency data received. Analysis complete. Your family resonance is currently at optimal alignment. You are beating at exactly the same frequency as the temporal fractures. This is both success and critical complication. You no longer repel the Cascade. You now attract it. You are beginning to stabilize it, but you are also beginning to become it. Proceed to Córdoba's central plaza. The next measurement point will be there. Time is accelerating. Twenty days may not be enough.*

Aaliyah showed the message to her mother, who read it with the expression of someone whose journalistic instincts were screaming that something fundamental had just shifted in their understanding of the problem.

"We're not separate from the glitches anymore," Luisana said quietly. "We're part of the system. We're beating in sync with it. Which means—"

"Which means when we stop," Aaliyah finished, "the glitches stop. Which means when our hearts stop beating, the world stops breaking because we're literally providing the rhythm that's keeping the fractures open."

"Is that good or bad?" Elijah asked. He was petting Sully with absolute concentration, like the dog could explain the cosmic implications through the medium of fur texture.

"I don't know yet," Aaliyah admitted.

The cathedral rose window was now so bright she could barely look at it directly. The colors had shifted into a spectrum she'd never seen in any normal light—frequencies so pure they seemed to exist in more than three dimensions. She could see every moment her family had loved each other, every decision they'd made without planning it, every argument and laugh and quiet morning and loud adventure. All of it mapped out in light, all of it translating into frequency, all of it broadcasting the coordinates of their own hearts into a broken timeline.

"We should go," Anthony said. He'd appeared next to his daughter and was looking at the window with an expression of profound tenderness. "If there's another measurement point, we should go measure ourselves again. We should know if we're getting stronger or if we're running out of time."

The question hung in the cathedral light.

Aaliyah looked at her family—her mother still writing observations, her father ready to move toward whatever came next, her brother communing with their dog about the nature of temporal resonance, and Sully himself, who'd apparently decided that being a glitch detector was the best possible career path for a golden retriever.

They were a heartbeat.

They were a frequency.

They were becoming something that wasn't quite human and wasn't quite part of the Cascade.

Something in between.

"Let's go," she said.

The morpho butterfly landed on the rose window as they turned to leave, its iridescent blue wings catching the colored light and reflecting it back into the cathedral in a way that suggested Abuela Ida was still paying very close attention to how this all turned out.

Nineteen days remaining.

And for the first time, Aaliyah wasn't sure which side of that countdown they were racing toward.
